title: Services, value added (% of GDP)
definition: Services industries correspond to ISIC (Rev. 4) divisions 45-99 and includes wholesale and retail trade, repair of motor vehicles, hotels and retaurants, transport, storage and communication, financial intermediation, real estate, renting and business activities, public administration and defence, compulsory social security, education, health and social work, other community, social and personal service activities, private households with employed persons, and extra-territorial organizations and bodies. Value added is the contribution to the economy by a producer or an industry or an institutional sector, which is estimated by the total value of output produced and deducting the total value of intermediate consumption of goods and services used to produce that output. This indicator is expressed as a percentage of Gross Domestic Product (GDP) which is the total income earned through the production of goods and services in an economic territory during an accounting period.

source.license: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International unless specifically labeled otherwise by World Bank metadata

editorial.patternNote: The darkest countries include Luxembourg, Bahamas, The, Cyprus, while the lightest include Guyana, Libya, Tanzania; that contrast is the fastest way to read the services value added pattern.
editorial.whyItMatters: Services value added shows how much national output comes from trade, finance, government, tourism, and other services.

bestProbeCountries.reason: High anchor: this country sits near the darkest end of the map.
Low anchor: this country clarifies the lightest end of the scale.
Middle check: this country helps separate a true pattern from one-off outliers.

commonConfusions.reason: Employment in services can tempt players because it shares a strong country ranking with this map, but its unit and definition point to a different story.
Health spending per person can tempt players because it shares a strong country ranking with this map, but its unit and definition point to a different story.
Safely managed drinking water can tempt players because it shares a strong country ranking with this map, but its unit and definition point to a different story.

editorial.difficultyReason: Standard difficulty: the map has readable regional structure, but the best answer still depends on checking the unit and country anchors.
regionalSignals: Economic-share maps are not the same as raw totals.
Small open economies can dominate percentage maps.

reviewNotes: Useful Practice map, but often acts like the inverse of agriculture/industry structure.
